---
title: "Progress Check 2 Review Session"
author:
  - Elizabeth King
  - Kevin Middleton
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    standalone: true
    self-contained: true
    logo: QMLS_Logo.png
    slide-number: true
    show-slide-number: all
code-annotations: hover
bibliography: QMLS_Bibliography.bib
csl: evolution.csl
---

```{r}
library(readxl)
library(tidyverse)
library(patchwork)
library(cowplot)
library(performance)
library(see)

theme_set(theme_cowplot(font_size = 18))
```

## Topics for Today

- PC 2 clarifications
- Linear model interpretations
- Transformations
- PS 8, Loops


## PC 2 Clarifications

> On the t-test assumption check, I assume we were supposed to use vars.equal = T for the first example? The second example asks us to use Welch's, but the instructions don't say to assume equal variance on the first example.

Yes.


## PC 2 Clarifications

> How are we supposed to run the linear model on hypothesis 3 of the mice example? I know that we can use y ~ a + b, but the thing is, there are two completely different relationships, and I don't feel like the logic of that make sense. I want to run different linear models on them, and now I'm realizing that might make more sense? If I find a different effect of body mass on two separate groups, would that be fine?

See Unit 8 naked mole-rats example.


## Reproducible research

> I would like suggestions on how to keep track of which statistical tests you have done and the logical flow that can help you move along in the process.


## Linear model interpretations

> I could use a refresher on stat summary tables and interpretation of outputs with the data (like R^2 vs p-value, F vs T, etc.), or any more examples could be helpful.


## Parts of a linear model summary

![](https://i.imgur.com/P8034XE.jpg){fig-align="center" width=90%}


## Parts of a linear model summary

![](https://i.imgur.com/38J8BwL.jpg){fig-align="center" width=90%}


## Parts of a linear model summary

![](https://i.imgur.com/oIqWNJg.jpg){fig-align="center" width=90%}


## Transformations

> Since there are a variety of transformations that can be used, is there a "gold standard" per se? Does it depend on the data, and is it recommended to test out a few?

> One thing that tripped me up during problem set 8 was the heart transplantation activity, where we had to transform survival_days to correct for unequal variances before running an ANOVA. I ended up using a log transformation because that is the main transformation we have practiced, but I do not fully understand how transforming the data helps satisfy the assumptions of a linear model. I may be overthinking it, but I am still confused about why this works and how to decide which transformation is most appropriate.


## Transformations for normality

```{r}
Tri <- read_excel("../data/NHANES_Triglycerides.xlsx") |>
  filter(Sex == "Female")

p1 <- ggplot(Tri, aes(Waist)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(x = "Waist Circumference (cm)", y = "Count")

p2 <- ggplot(Tri, aes(Triglycerides)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(x = "Serum Triglycerides (mg/dL)", y = "Count")

p1 + p2
```


## Transformations for normality

```{r}
ggplot(Tri, aes(x = Waist, y = Triglycerides)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(
    formula = y ~ x,
    method = "lm",
    se = FALSE,
    color = "firebrick4"
  ) +
  labs(x = "Waist Circumference (cm)", y = "Serum Triglycerides (mg/dL)")
```


## Diagnostics

```{r}
fm1 <- lm(Triglycerides ~ Waist, data = Tri)
PP <- plot(check_model(fm1, panel = FALSE))
PP[[1]]
```


## Linearity

```{r}
PP[[2]]
```


## Homogeneity of variance

```{r}
PP[[3]]
```


## Influential observations

```{r}
PP[[4]]
```


## Normality of residuals

```{r}
PP[[5]]
```


## Model diagnostics

> Besides the traditional visual evaluation through diagnostic plots, are there any formal statistical tests that you would recommend for checking model assumptions in an lm?

- Treat diagnostics as information.
- You are in charge of your analysis.


## Additional checks

```{r}
#| echo: true

check_heteroskedasticity(fm1)
check_normality(fm1)
check_outliers(fm1)
```


## A "Ladder" of Transformations

![](https://zief0002.github.io/bespectacled-antelope/notes/figs/ladder-of-transformations.png){fig-align="center"}

[The rule of the bulge](https://zief0002.github.io/bespectacled-antelope/notes/09-rule-of-the-bulge.html)


## Original data

```{r}
p2
```


## Transformations for normality

```{r}
Tri |>
  mutate(
    `sqrt(x)` = sqrt(Triglycerides),
    `ln(x)` = log(Triglycerides),
    `1 / x` = 1 / (Triglycerides^1),
    `1 / x^2` = 1 / (Triglycerides^2)
  ) |>
  select(7:10) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = everything()) |>
  mutate(name = fct_inorder(name)) |>
  ggplot(aes(value)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "steelblue") +
  facet_grid(name ~ .) +
  labs(y = "Count")
```


## Transformed Triglycerides

```{r}
Tri <- Tri |> mutate(log_Tri = log(Triglycerides))

ggplot(Tri, aes(x = Waist, y = log_Tri)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue") +
  labs(x = "Waist Circumference (cm)", y = "ln Serum Triglycerides (mg/dL)")

```


## Diagnostics

```{r}
fm1 <- lm(log_Tri ~ Waist, data = Tri)
PP <- plot(check_model(fm1, panel = FALSE))
PP[[1]]
```


## Linearity

```{r}
PP[[2]]
```


## Homogeneity of variance

```{r}
PP[[3]]
```


## Influential observations

```{r}
PP[[4]]
```


## Normality of residuals

```{r}
PP[[5]]
```


## Additional checks

```{r}
#| echo: true

check_heteroskedasticity(fm1)
check_normality(fm1)
check_outliers(fm1)
```

## Multicollinearity

> I feel like I know that it's bad when predictors are correlated with one another and what the model out put looks like, but I don't know what to do to fix it should I encounter it. Do I just drop a predictor, or do seperate analysis?

> In general I think I am still struggling with being able to read the stat summaries and draw conclusions from them. More specifically, I am confused about to multiple variable correlations. Can we go over them again?


## Factor levels

> I am having difficulty with setting the base level of the factor in the first part of the problem set. Should I use fct_relevel()? Could we please go over an example of how to tackle this?


## Factor levels

```{r}
#| echo: true

Tri <- read_excel("../data/NHANES_Triglycerides.xlsx") |>
  mutate(Sex = factor(Sex))
levels(Tri$Sex)
```


## `fct_relevel()`

```{r}
#| echo: true

Tri <- Tri |> mutate(Sex = fct_relevel(Sex, "Male"))
levels(Tri$Sex)
```


## Factor levels

```{r}
#| echo: true

Tri <- read_excel("../data/NHANES_Triglycerides.xlsx") |>
  mutate(Sex = factor(Sex, levels = c("Male", "Female")))
levels(Tri$Sex)
```


## `fct_....` functions

- `fct_relevel()`: Rearrange
- `fct_collapse()`: Combine levels
- `fct_drop()`: Drop unused levels
- `fct_infreq()`: Order by frequency
- `fct_inorder()`: Order by appearance
- `fct_rev()`: Reverse the existing order of levels


## PS 8

> I am not sure I understand the purpose/application of pmap_dbl function, would mutate accomplish the same thing?

## Loops

> I think for loops make sense in theory, but in coding I still get stuck with the brackets and placement of lines inside the loop.

> When to use a loop, apply, or pmap?

> I understand loops, but functions are still a headache, especially when we have a loop within a function. Can we go over function syntax?
